title: The Paper Gown and the Psoas: Navigating Healthcare System Rage
dek: *The examination room transforms us. Behind that flimsy curtain, wearing nothing but industrial cotton and uncertainty, our bodies remember every dismissal, every rushed diagnosis, every time we were told our pain was probably stress. The psoas knows what we cannot yet say.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Healthcare encounters often trigger rage that lodges in the psoas muscle, creating a somatic signature of powerlessness. The paper gown is not just clothing but a ritual that strips agency and primes the body for freeze response. Learning to track and release this specific anger through muscle awareness can restore both physical ease and personal authority.

She sat on the crinkly paper in the fluorescent cold, her feet dangling like a child's, the gown gaping open at the back. The doctor had been seven minutes late, then interrupted her third sentence to type notes she couldn't see. She smiled. She nodded. She said "thank you" when he stood to leave, prescription in hand for something she hadn't asked about. Her jaw was relaxed. Her voice was warm. But deep in her pelvis, in the rope of muscle that connects her spine to her femur, something coiled tighter, a fist she didn't know she was making. ## The Geography of Medical Powerlessness The psoas major is the only muscle that connects your spine to your legs. It runs from your twelfth thoracic vertebra through your pelvis to your inner thigh, passing directly beside your kidneys and reproductive organs. When you walk, it lifts your leg. When you sit too long, it shortens. When you feel trapped and cannot flee, it clenches. In my own practice, I've noticed that people who describe chronic psoas tension almost always have stories about medical encounters where they felt unheard. The muscle becomes a filing cabinet for experiences the conscious mind has labeled as "fine" but the body refuses to forget. You leave the appointment. You drive home. You make dinner. And three days later your lower back seizes, or your hip clicks, or you can't quite take a full breath. This is not metaphor. Research has documented the physiological cost of suppressed anger in healthcare settings. A PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load demonstrates that inhibiting anger expression during interpersonal stress significantly raises both blood pressure and cortisol. The body pays for the smile you wear when what you need is to say "stop talking and listen." The paper gown is the costume of this transaction. It hangs open. It exposes. It rustles when you shift, announcing your discomfort to anyone passing in the hall. Harriet Lerner writes in *The Dance of Anger* about how women are socialized to manage others' emotions at the expense of their own clarity, and the medical encounter is perhaps the most theatrical staging of this dynamic. You are cold, undressed, elevated on a table, while a fully clothed authority figure controls the pace, the questions, the diagnosis, and the exit. ## The Ritual Stripping of Agency There's a moment that happens in every examination room, right after you've changed into the gown but before the doctor arrives. You sit. You wait. You become aware of the thinness of the fabric, the institutional smell, the hum of ventilation. Your autonomy has been folded neatly with your clothes and placed on the chair. Thomas Moore suggests in *Care of the Soul* that our modern medical system has lost the capacity for sacred witnessing, replacing it with efficiency protocols and risk management. The paper gown is the uniform of the profane, the opposite of ceremonial dress. It confers no protection, no dignity, no power. It marks you as patient, which is to say, as the one who waits, who endures, who receives judgment. You came because something hurts. You came because you need help. And the first act of the encounter is to make you more vulnerable. This is by design, though not necessarily by conspiracy. The system is built for speed, for liability reduction, for data capture. Your body is an input. Your story is a symptom cluster to be coded. And so the psoas braces. It prepares for impact that may or may not come. It holds the memory of the last time, and the time before that, when you said "this doesn't feel right" and were met with a shrug, a prescription, a referral to someone else. The muscle becomes the autobiography of your dismissals. ## When Anger Goes Underground Soraya Chemaly's *Rage Becomes Her* documents how girls are taught from early childhood to prioritize being liked over being heard, to fear anger as a threat to relationships rather than as information about boundaries. In healthcare settings, this training intensifies. The "difficult patient" is the one who questions, who brings research, who does not accept the first answer. The compliant patient gets better care, or so the implicit bargain suggests. So you swallow it. You thank the doctor who didn't listen. You fill the prescription you don't understand. You tell yourself you're being reasonable, collaborative, a good advocate for your own health. And the anger doesn't vanish. It migrates. Over the years I've seen this pattern repeat with remarkable consistency: the person who leaves the neurologist's office with a diagnosis of "stress" wakes the next morning unable to lift their right leg without hip pain. The person told their fatigue is "just depression" develops chronic lower back spasms. The body stages the protest the voice will not. The psoas is particularly eloquent in this language. Because it governs the fight-or-flight mechanism, tightness here signals unresolved threat. You couldn't run. You couldn't fight. So you froze, and the freeze is still happening, days or months later, in the core of your skeletal system. [Garden of Life Organics B-12 Spray (Methylcobalamin)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00K5NEPJY?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help with the nerve support that chronic tension demands, but no supplement will release what hasn't been acknowledged. ## The Somatic Signature of Institutional Rage Let's get specific about what this feels like. You're lying in bed at night. You roll to your side and feel a catch, a pulling sensation that runs from your lower ribs down through your groin. You stretch. It doesn't help. You foam-roll. The relief lasts ten minutes. You see a physical therapist who gives you exercises that address the muscle but not the memory. Because the psoas doesn't just hold tension. It holds narrative. It remembers the fluorescent light. The cold stirrups. The way the doctor's eyes stayed on the screen while you described your pain. The dismissive "everything looks normal" when nothing feels normal. The prescription for anxiety medication when what you needed was a blood panel. This is institutional rage, and it has a different flavor than personal anger. Personal anger has a target, a face, a specific grievance. Institutional rage is diffuse. It's directed at a system that means well and does harm, that employs good people within bad structures, that asks you to be grateful for access even when that access is conditional on your silence. In our experience, people often don't recognize this anger as anger. They call it frustration. They call it stress. They say "I'm just tired of dealing with this." But the body knows. The psoas clenches not because you're weak but because you're strong, because somewhere in your nervous system a part of you is still saying "this is not okay" even when your mouth has learned to say "thank you for your time." ## A Five-Minute Practice for Psoas Awareness Find a quiet space where you can lie on your back. Bend your knees, feet flat on the floor. Place one hand on your lower belly, just above your pubic bone. Place the other hand on your chest. Breathe normally and notice which hand moves more. Most people will feel more movement in the chest. This is the breathing pattern of mild, chronic activation. The psoas stays braced when we breathe shallowly. Now, without forcing, see if you can let the belly hand rise more with each inhale. Imagine you're breathing into the space behind your navel, down into your pelvis. As you exhale, let your lower back soften toward the floor. You're not pressing it down. You're allowing it to release. As you continue, bring to mind the last medical appointment that left you feeling unheard. Don't replay the whole story. Just notice where in your body you feel the memory. Often it will be the psoas area, that deep crease where your thigh meets your torso. Breathe into that space. On your exhale, imagine you can speak one true sentence you didn't say in that room. You don't have to say it aloud. Just let your body know you know. The anger is real. The dismissal happened. You're not making it up. Do this for five minutes. When you're done, roll to your side and rest there before standing. Notice if your hip feels different. Notice if your breath has changed. This is not catharsis. This is contact. ## The Herbal Allies for Held Rage There are plants that understand this particular knot. Cramp bark works on smooth muscle spasm, yes, but it also seems to address the emotional component of holding. Motherwort has an affinity for the heart that's been asked to stay open while under threat. Passionflower helps the nervous system remember that it's allowed to rest, even when vigilance has become a habit. You'll find some of these in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), though the real medicine is not in taking something but in acknowledging what you're taking it for. A tincture won't fix a broken system. But it can support your body while you learn to recognize your own anger as valid, as intelligent, as a form of self-protection that deserves respect rather than suppression. [Traditional Medicinals Throat Coat (16 bags)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00B77XTA8?tag=spankyspinola-20) has helped many people I've worked with, not because throat tension and psoas tension are the same, but because they're often linked. The voice that couldn't speak and the muscle that couldn't run are in conversation. Supporting one often eases the other. Magnesium is the mineral of letting go. The threonate form crosses the blood-brain barrier, which matters when the holding pattern is as much neurological as muscular. [Momentous Magtein Magnesium L-Threonate, 30 Servings](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B09WWK4R2C?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be part of a broader protocol for chronic tension, alongside the emotional work of recognizing what you've been carrying. ## Reclaiming the Examination Room Here's what changes when you start to track this anger consciously: you notice the moment the gown goes on and your breathing shifts. You feel the psoas brace when the doctor interrupts. You recognize the smile you're wearing as a costume, not a feeling. And from that recognition, choice becomes possible. Not the choice to rage at your healthcare provider, though sometimes direct confrontation is exactly right. But the choice to say, calmly and clearly, "I need you to listen to the whole description before we discuss treatment." The choice to write down your questions beforehand and refuse to leave until they're answered. The choice to change providers when dismissal is the pattern, not the exception. This is not about being difficult. This is about understanding that your anger in these settings is often proportional to the power imbalance, and that the imbalance is structural. The paper gown will always be thin. The table will always be high. But you can walk into that room knowing your body's response is not pathology but perception. Across the articles we've published on this site, a theme emerges: the anger we're most afraid of is usually the anger that's telling us the truth. The psoas doesn't lie. When it clenches in the presence of medical authority, it's not being dramatic. It's being accurate. Something about this encounter is not safe, even if nothing overtly dangerous is happening. ## The Wisdom of the Body's No There's a broader conversation happening at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) about the intelligence of embodied rage, how it functions as a guidance system rather than a problem to solve. The psoas is one of the most sensitive instruments in that system. It knows when you're in the presence of someone who sees you as a whole person and when you're being processed as a case file. Learning to trust that knowing is not the same as becoming hostile or defensive. It's becoming literate in your own somatic language. The tightness after an appointment is not random. The hip pain that flares when you schedule the follow-up is not coincidence. Your body is trying to tell you something about the quality of care you're receiving, about whether this relationship is actually serving your healing or just managing your symptoms. Some providers will welcome this awareness. They'll slow down when you ask them to. They'll explain the why behind the prescription. They'll sit rather than stand, make eye contact, ask what you think is happening. These are the ones worth keeping. The others, the ones who bristle at questions or hide behind jargon or make you feel stupid for not understanding, your psoas already knows what your loyalty hasn't admitted yet. ## Living Questions ### Why does my lower back hurt more after doctor visits, even when nothing physical happened? Your psoas muscle responds to perceived threat by contracting, preparing your body to flee or fight. In medical settings where you feel unheard or dismissed, this response activates but has nowhere to go. You can't run from the examination table, and you can't fight the power dynamic, so the muscle stays contracted. Hours or days later, that sustained tension manifests as pain. The physical symptom is real, even though the original trigger was emotional and relational. ### Is it normal to feel angry at doctors who are trying to help? Completely normal, and often accurate. Intention doesn't negate impact. A provider can mean well and still dismiss your symptoms, rush your appointment, or fail to see you as the expert on your own body. Your anger is information about a mismatch between what you need and what you're receiving. It doesn't make you ungrateful. It makes you awake to the reality of the interaction. The question is not whether the anger is justified but what you'll do with the information it's giving you. ### Can working with psoas tension actually change how I navigate healthcare? Yes, because muscle awareness builds body literacy, and body literacy builds confidence. When you can feel the moment your psoas braces, you have real-time feedback about your nervous system's assessment of safety. That feedback can guide decisions: whether to speak up in the moment, whether to seek a second opinion, whether to leave a practice that consistently activates your threat response. The muscle work and the advocacy work are not separate. They're two aspects of reclaiming authority over your own care. ## The Paper Trail and the Muscle Memory There's a file somewhere with your name on it. It contains codes and notes and test results, a version of your body translated into billable language. That file matters. It determines coverage, referrals, continuity of care. But it's not the whole story. The whole story lives in your psoas, in the way your breath catches when you pull into the clinic parking lot, in the dreams you have the night before the appointment where you're trying to speak but no sound comes out. This story doesn't fit on the intake form. It doesn't have a diagnostic code. But it's the one that determines whether you'll actually follow through with treatment, whether you'll trust the process, whether healing is even possible within this particular relationship. You can honor both stories. You can show up to appointments with your questions written down and your anger acknowledged. You can wear the paper gown and still know your own authority. The muscle will still brace sometimes. That's okay. What matters is that you notice, that you breathe, that you remember the tightness is not happening *to* you but *for* you, a loyal alarm system doing exactly what it was designed to do. For more context on how we approach these questions, you might explore [the about page](/about) or browse [more articles in this journal](/articles) that track the intersection of body and voice, muscle and meaning, the places where silence costs us more than speaking ever could. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on the paper gown and the psoas: navigating healthcare system rage, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
